serving.description: 1 shared hotpot portion (approx. 400 g per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving is approximately 400 g, representing an individual portion from a shared hotpot that includes broth, pork, meatballs, tofu, beancurd sticks, and pickled vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Pork hotpot / Chinese mixed meat steamboat — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (pork belly, tofu, beancurd, pickled vegetables, broth) and HPB Singapore nutrient reference for steamboat dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy); Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for chrysanthemum-pot mixed meat hotpot. Values are estimated by weighted-average component analysis: pork belly (~30%), pork meatballs (~20%), tofu (~15%), dried beancurd sticks (~10%), pickled vegetables (~10%), and savory broth (~15%), using USDA SR Legacy entries for each component and cross-referenced against HPB Singapore steamboat dish references. Per-100g values reflect the edible mixed dish including broth absorption.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and tofu hotpot estimated from analogous low-GI components: tofu (GI ~15), pork (GI ~0, protein-fat dominant), beancurd/soy products (GI ~20–25), pickled vegetables (GI ~15); weighted composite estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database soy and legume entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chrysanthemum-pot mixed meat hotpot. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on weighted contribution of primary carbohydrate-bearing ingredients: tofu (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database), dried beancurd sticks/soy protein (GI ~20–25, University of Sydney GI Database), pickled vegetables (GI ~15, low-GI vegetable reference), and pork/meatballs (negligible GI contribution as protein-fat dominant). Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (32 × 4.3 g carbohydrate × 4) / 100 = 5.5 g, classified low. This estimate is conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per 400 g serving) from savory broth, pickled vegetables, and processed meatballs
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid drinking the broth, as a single 400 g serving may contribute approximately 73% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2300 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth and minimizing pickled vegetable intake is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) driven by tofu, beancurd sticks, and protein-fat dominant pork components; moderate carbohydrate content (~4.3 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This hotpot has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load per serving (~5.5 g), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added rice or noodles; the high protein and fat content further blunts postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g per 100 g) from pork belly and meatballs, combined with high sodium from broth and pickled vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to saturated fat and sodium content; choosing leaner cuts, reducing broth consumption, and limiting pickled vegetables can help manage cardiovascular risk while still enjoying this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Meat Hotpot (菊花炉肉暖锅, Chrysanthemum Pot Hotpot) is a traditional Chinese communal stew dish originating from the Qing Dynasty, served in Singapore at Chinese restaurants and hotpot establishments. The dish is prepared in a distinctive chrysanthemum-shaped brass or copper pot, in which a rich savory broth is used to slowly stew and simmer pork belly, pork meatballs, firm tofu, dried beancurd sticks (fu zhu), and pickled vegetables (suan cai). The result is a deeply flavored, umami-rich dish with tender meat, silky tofu, and slightly tangy pickled vegetables that balance the richness of the pork. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (~9.2 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (~7.1 g per 100 g), with a significant sodium load (~420 mg per 100 g) primarily from the broth and pickled components. The glycemic index is low (~32) due to the dominance of protein, fat, and soy-based ingredients, making it relatively suitable for glycemic management when consumed without starchy accompaniments. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium intake for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, as a full serving can contribute substantially to daily sodium limits. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains soy allergens from tofu and beancurd sticks. It is a culturally significant dish representing Qing Dynasty imperial hotpot traditions adapted for modern Singaporean Chinese dining.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for chrysanthemum-pot mixed meat hotpot as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy and HPB Singapore steamboat references. GI is estimated from analogous soy and protein-dominant components via the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level evidence is strong and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 because it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimated dish; a reliable per-100g value cannot be calculated from the available approved sources. All other per_100g fields are numeric estimates derived from weighted component analysis. No core macros are zero. GI is estimated at 32 (low) from analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy and protein-dominant components; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Price data (SGD 49.40) is preserved from the field survey data source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. The restaurant 'See Ya Tonight 今晚有局' serves this dish at Singapore Level 1. All aliases_by_language string values have been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
